title: Notes on Geometric Perception for Large-Scale Robotic Systems
summary: A short reflection on why geometric consistency, calibration, and uncertainty matter when perception systems leave controlled lab environments.

content.text: Large-scale robotic perception is often described through sensors and algorithms, but the practical bottleneck is usually geometric consistency. A system may collect dense point clouds, accurate poses, or high-resolution images, yet the final reconstruction depends on whether all observations share a reliable spatial reference.
For example, a rigid transformation can be written inline as \( T \in SE(3) \), which is compact but still makes the assumed motion model explicit.

This is especially visible in aircraft surface reconstruction, mobile scanning, and structured field environments. A representative setup is shown in [[fig:measurement-pipeline]]. Small calibration errors can become visible as global deformation, misalignment, or unstable downstream estimation.

content.alt: Aircraft surface reconstruction workflow with measurement equipment and point cloud results.
content.caption: A representative large-scale aircraft surface measurement workflow, where geometric consistency across sensing devices determines the quality of the final reconstruction.

content.text: A simple registration objective can be expressed as \[ \min_{R,t} \sum_i \left\| \mathbf{p}_i - (R\mathbf{q}_i + t) \right\|^2 \], where the residuals summarize point-wise geometric consistency.
Different sensing contexts often share the same geometric concerns while exposing them through distinct surface structures, as illustrated in [[fig:surface-comparison]].

content.caption: Two large-scale perception scenarios where calibration quality strongly affects downstream reconstruction consistency.

items: Treat calibration as part of the perception pipeline, not as a one-time preprocessing step.
Record coordinate frames, transformation directions, and units explicitly in code and experiment logs.
Evaluate global consistency with independent checks instead of relying only on local residuals.

content.text: In practice, I find the sanity checks in [[tbl:consistency-checks]] more useful than adding one more local residual metric, because they reveal whether the whole pipeline shares a stable spatial reference.

rows: Prevent sign errors and inverted transforms

rows.text: Point clouds align locally but drift globally.
Keep calibration, reconstruction, and evaluation in the same scale.
Pose estimates appear correct, but the error term \( \|\mathbf{e}\|_2 \) becomes misleading across stages.

rows: Verify the reconstruction outside the optimization objective
The system overfits the chosen metric and hides structural bias
content.caption: A compact set of checks for maintaining geometric consistency throughout a large-scale perception workflow.

content.text: A useful research direction is to connect geometric optimization with uncertainty-aware decision making. Instead of producing a single fused result, the system can expose where the reconstruction is reliable and where additional observations are needed.
